---
title: "Winter Olympics Medal Analysis"
author: "Octavia Steiger"
date: today

format:
    html:
        theme: cosmo
        highlight-style: github
        toc: true
        toc-title: 'Contents'
        toc-depth: 2
        code-fold: true
        code-summary: 'Show code'
        fig-cap-location: bottom
    pdf:
        toc: true

execute:
    warning: false
    message: false

jupyter: python3
---

## Why does Norway win so much?
Norway has a population of just 5 million people, yet it sits at the top of the all-time Winter Olympic medal table with 318 medals, more than 40 ahead of the United States despite the US having around 60 times the population. So what is actually happening?

This project sets out to answer that question using the data from every Winter Olympic Games since Chamonix 1924. The analysis tests three possible explanations, wealth (richer countries invest more in training and facilities), population size (more people means more potential athletes) and home advantage (hosting the Games gives a measurable boost). The answer turns out to be yes to all three but not equally.

:::{.callout-note}
## The short answer
Yes to all three, but not equally. Wealth and population size are the strongest predictors of medal success, while hosting provides a measurable but smaller boost above and beyond those factors.
:::

Before diving into the results, it helps to understand exactly what data sits behind this analysis and how it was built.

*The pipeline: scrape --> clean --> merge with World Bank data --> analyse --> visualise*

<hr>

## 1. The data
This project draws on two datasets, one tracking Olympic medal outcomes going back a century, and one capturing the economic context behind each competing nation. 

#### Olympic Medals (Olympedia): 
Scraped from [Olympedia](https://www.olympedia.org/), covering all 24 Winter Games from Chamonix 1924 to Beijing 2022. Each row represents one country's performance in a single Games (1,163 country-year observations in total).

| Variable | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `NOC` | National Olympic Committee country code |
| `Year` | Olympic Year (1924 - 2022) |
| `Gold / Silver / Bronze` | Medal counts for that Games |
| `Total` | Sum of all medals won |
| `Host` | 1 if the country hosted that year, 0 otherwise |

#### Economic Indicators (World Bank):
GDP per capita (Current USD, indicator `NY.GDP.PCAP.CD`) and population (`SP.POP.TOTL`) fetched via the World Bank API, then matched to each country-year in the medals dataset.

> **A note on tied medals:** In 27 events where multiple athletes tied for the same medal position, each tied nation received full medal credit, consistent with how the IOC handles ties in official records.

With the dataset defined, the next section explains how it was built, from raw web pages through to a clean, merged file ready for analysis.

<hr>

## 2. Building the dataset
### 2.1 Collecting the Medal Data
The first step was to collect the medal data for each of the Winter Olympic Games all the way back to 1924. Official Olympic data was not available as a download, so the data had to be scraped directly from Olympedia (https://www.olympedia.org). 

I built a web scraper (`scrape.py`) that fetched the medal table for each of the 24 Winter Olympic Games, making sure that I paused between requests to avoid overwhelming the server. Each page returned an HTML table listing every event alongside gold, silver and bronze medalists and their countries. 

The raw data came back in a wide format, with separate columns for gold, silver and bronze medalists. At this point the data was still quite messy, with country names in different formats, sports categories mixed in as heading rows and some NOC fields had combined codes for when medals were tied. All of this needed cleaning up before any meaningful analysis could start.

The dataset spans 24 Games from Chamonix 1924 to Beijing 2022, covering 1163 unique events. Not all NOCs appear in each Games, the number of competing nations has grown over time, from around 19 nations in 1924 to over 90 today. 

```{python}
import pandas as pd
medals = pd.read_csv('../data/clean/medals_clean.csv')
print(f"{len(medals)} events scraped across {medals['year'].nunique()} Games")
medals.head(10)
```

### 2.2 Cleaning the Data

#### Sorting out the sport categories
The scraped HTML table included two types of rows: actual event results and section headings that just said the sport name. These headings were not real results, they had no medalists data or NOC codes, however they did have the sport name of each event which is information I still needed. 

To fix this, within the cleaning script (`clean.py`) I identified the heading rows which was any row where all medal columns were empty and then extracted the sport name. Allowing me to forward fill the sport name down to all the event rows below it until reaching the next heading. Then I could drop the original heading rows as they were no longer needed.
```{python}
print("Sports present in the dataset:")
print(sorted(medals['sport'].dropna().unique().tolist()))
```

#### Dealing with tied medals
Occasionally there were two countries that shared a podium position, for example two gold medalists and no silver. In the raw data these appear as a single combined entry in the NOC column rather than as two seperate rows. 

These needed to be detected and split into two rows, otherwise any country-level analysis would be inaccurate. Within the cleaning step these are flagged using the `tie` columns and then are split into seperate rows in a later step in the pipeline, so that both countries get the credit for the medal in the analysis.

```{python}
tied = medals[medals['tie_gold'] | medals['tie_silver'] | medals['tie_bronze']]
print(f"Events with at least one tied medal: {len(tied)}")
tied[['year', 'sport', 'event', 'tie_gold', 'tie_silver', 'tie_bronze']].head(8)
```

There are relatively few tied medals within the data however just ignoring them would mean undercounting the total medals for the countries involved and would lead to skewed results from the analysis.
:::{.callout-tip}
## Why this matters
Ignoring ties would undercount medals for countries involved. Splitting them into seperate rows ensures every nation gets full credit which is consistent with how the IOC handles ties in official records.
:::

### 2.3 Adding Economic Context
Medal counts alone only tell part of the story, I also need to add some economic context to the medal data. To test whether wealth or population drives success, each medal row needed to be merged with GDP and population data for the corresponding year.

#### Why I used the World Bank API
I needed two indicators for each country and year: GDP per capita and population size. The World Bank API provided a convenient way to access this data for all the countries and years in my medal dataset. The advantage of using the World Bank for both is that the data is already cleaned and standardised, meaning I didn't have to worry about different country name formats or different structures for the data. 

They were both fetched directly from the World Bank API (https://data.worldbank.org/) using the `wbgapi` Python package, allowing for easy integration into the data pipeline.

#### The NOC to ISC problem
There was however a problem with merging the medal data with the World Bank data, as the medal data uses NOC codes to identify countries, whereas the World Bank data uses ISO country codes. This meant that I needed to find a way to map between the two code systems in order to merge the datasets together as well as the problem of some NOCs no longer existing or being split into multiple countries over time. So I had to create a lookup table to map each NOC to its closest modern equivalent, so that historical medals can still be joined to economic data where possible. 

The trickiest cases were the Soviet Union (URS), East Germany (GDR) and Yugoslavia (YUG) which are all heavy medal winners that no longer exist. For these I mapped to their largest successor state (Russia, Germany and Serbia) where economic data was available and excluded rows where I could not make a reasonable match. 

#### Merging the datasets
Once both datasets used the same country codes, they were joined on country and year. The result was a single dataset combining medal outcomes with economic indicators for every country-year, which is the foundation for the regression analysis. Alongside this I created two additional columns: 

- **`is_host`** : a flag marking whether the winning country was hosting the games that year, to test the impact of home advantage 
- **`log_gdp_per_capita`** and **`log_population`**: I took the log of both GDP per Capita and population to make the data more normally distributed, as the difference between a very poor and a moderately poor country is much more significant than the difference between a moderately rich and a very rich country, so taking the log allows for a more meaningful analysis of the relationship between wealth and medal success.

```{python}
final = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/worldbank_final.csv")
print(f"Final merged dataset: {len(final)} medal rows")
final.head(8)
```
```{python}
missing = final['gdp_per_capita'].isna().sum()
total = len(final)
print(f'Rows with missing GDP per capita: {missing} ({100 * missing / total:.1f}%)')
print('These are mostly historic NOCs')
```

The missing GDP rows are expected and unavoidable, as many of the older NOCs no longer exist and therefore do not have corresponding economic data in the World Bank dataset. Therefore, these rows are excluded from the regression but kept in the raw medal count so the all-time table remains accurate.

:::{.callout-warning}
## Data limitation
Around 16% of medal rows are missing GDP data, mostly from historical NOCs that no longer exist (URS, GDR, YUG). These are excluded from the regression but kept in the all-time medal counts so historical totals remain accurate.
:::

With a clean, merged dataset in hand, the first question is simply: Who has dominanted the Winter Olympics over the past century, and has that changed over time?

<hr> 

## 3. A century of dominance
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid', font_scale=1.1)
GOLD   = "#FFD700"
SILVER = "#C0C0C0"
BRONZE = "#CD7F32"
BLUE   = "#1f77b4"
alltime = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/alltime_table.csv")
cy      = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/medals_country_year.csv")
reg     = pd.read_csv("../outputs/regression_results.csv")
final   = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/worldbank_final.csv")
```

To get a sense of the scale of dominance before looking at the charts, the table below shows the 15 most decorated nations in Winter Olympic history, ranked by total medals won.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
summary = pd.read_csv('../outputs/summary_stats.csv', index_col=0)
summary.style.set_caption('Table 1: Top 15 nations by total Winter Olympic Medals')
```

Norway and the United States lead on total medals, both attending all 17 Games in the dataset. However, the average medals per Games column tells a more interesting story, by sorting by average medals rather than totals it reshuffles the rankings considerably.

:::{.callout-note}
## Germany and the Cold War nations
Germany's average of 24.4 medals per Games is the highest in the table desipte having only 11 appearances. This highlights how dominant West Germany and later reunified Germany have been when they competed. Russia (6 Games, 19.7 avg) and East Germany (6 Games, 18.3 avg) show even higher rates, however both stop competing after the Soviet era ended.
:::

The chart below shows cumulative medals since 1924 across the top 20 nations. Norway leads comfortably, which makes sense given how deeply embedded winter sport is in Norwegian culture, skiing is almost a national pastime. What's also interesting is how much of Norway's bar is gold rather than silver or bronze, showing it doesn't just compete but consistently wins. The US and Germany follow, both benefiting from their size and long-term investment in winter sport. 
```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Figure 1: Cumulative Winter Olympic medals for the top 20 nations since 1924. Norway's gold-heavy bar reflects not just volume but consistent dominance across disciplines."
#| label: fig-alltime
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
ax.barh(alltime['noc'], alltime['gold_medals'], label='Gold', color=GOLD, height=0.6)
ax.barh(alltime['noc'], alltime['silver_medals'], label='Silver', color=SILVER, height=0.6, left=alltime['gold_medals'])
ax.barh(alltime['noc'], alltime['bronze_medals'], label='Bronze', color=BRONZE, height=0.6, left=alltime['gold_medals'] + alltime['silver_medals'])
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_title("All time Winter Olympics Medal Table (top 20)", fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Total Medals Won")
ax.set_ylabel("Country")
ax.legend(loc='lower right')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The all-time totals hides a lot of movement at the individual Games level, so this chart tracks medals per Games for the top 6 nations, and the picture is far less static than the bar chart above suggests. 
```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Figure 2: Medals won per Games for the top 6 nations by all-time count. The gap between 1936 and 1948 reflects the cancellation of the 1940 and 1944 Games due to World War II. You’ll also notice a clear spike for the United States in 2002, which lines up with the Salt Lake City Games being held on home soil."
#| label: fig-overtime

top6   = cy.groupby('noc')['total_medals'].sum().nlargest(6).index.tolist()
subset = cy[cy['noc'].isin(top6)]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.lineplot(data=subset, x='year', y='total_medals',
             hue='noc', marker='o', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Winter Olympic Medal Count per Games (Top 6 Nations)',
             fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Medals Won')
ax.legend(title='Country', bbox_to_anchor=(1.01, 1),
          loc='upper left', borderaxespad=0.)
ax.set_xticks(sorted(cy['year'].unique()))
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Norway is consistently near the top throughout, with very few poor Games. Russia (URS before 1992) came on strong from the mid-1950s and dominated throughout the 1980s but then dropped off sharply after the Soviet Union dissolved. The dominance of wealthy nations raises the obvious next question: Is it just money?

<hr>

## 4. Does money buy medals?
The scatter plot below tests the wealth hypothesis directly, plotting log GDP per capita against total medals won across all country-year observations. The upward trend is clear, wealthier countries tend to win more medals, consistent with the idea that richer nations have more resources to invest in training, facilities and athlete development. 

:::{.callout-tip}
## Wealth isn't everything
Qatar and Singapore rank among the world's wealthiest nations but have never won a Winter Olympic medal, so climate and sporting culture clearly matter alongside money. The regression captures the average relationship across all nations, but it doesn't explain every single case.
:::

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Figure 3: Relationship between log GDP per capita and total medals won across all country-year observations. The overall upward trend makes it clear that richer countries tend to win more medals. That said, the spread of points around the line is a reminder that wealth isn't the whole story, other factors still matter."
#| label: fig-wealth
plot_data = cy.dropna(subset=['log_gdp_per_capita', 'total_medals'])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.regplot(data=plot_data, x='log_gdp_per_capita', y='total_medals',
            scatter_kws={'alpha': 0.4, 's': 25, 'color': BLUE},
            line_kws={'color': 'red'}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Wealthier Countries Win More Medals", fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Log GDP per Capita")
ax.set_ylabel("Total Medals Won")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Wealth clearly plays a role, however, there is one advantage no amount of investment can buy and that is competing on home soil. The next section tests whether that actually shows up in the data.

<hr>

## 5. The home advantage
To look at home advantage more directly, I compared each host nations medal count in the year they hosted against their average in all other Games, keeping the 15 nations that won the most medals whilst hosting.
```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Figure 4: Medal counts in host years versus average non-host performance for the 15 most successful host nations. Most sit noticeably higher in their host year, for example the USA in 2002 (34 medals) and Norway in 1994 (26 medals)."
#| label: fig-host
hosts = (cy[cy['is_host'] == 1][['noc', 'year', 'total_medals']].rename(columns={'total_medals': 'host_medals'}))
non_host_avg = (cy[cy['is_host'] == 0].groupby('noc')['total_medals'].mean().reset_index().rename(columns={'total_medals': 'avg_non_host'}))
compare = (pd.merge(hosts, non_host_avg, on='noc', how='inner').sort_values('host_medals', ascending=False).head(15))
long = compare.melt(id_vars=['noc'],value_vars=['host_medals', 'avg_non_host'], var_name='type', value_name='medals')
long['type'] = long['type'].map({'host_medals':  'Host Year','avg_non_host': 'Average Non-Host Year'})
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
sns.barplot(data=long, x='noc', y='medals', hue='type', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Medals when Hosting vs Not Hosting (Top 15 Host Nations)',fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Country')
ax.set_ylabel('Total Medals')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Most nations sit noticeably higher in the host year than their non-host average. The USA in 2002 and Norway in 1994 are both good examples of this. One thing to note is the chart orders by how many medals they won while hosting not by the size of the boost, so the nations on the left aren't necessarily the ones who benefitted most.

Home advantage shows up clearly in the data, but it is a one time boost. It cannot explain the dramatic collapse in performance of nations like the Soviet Union and East Germany, for that we need to look at the political and historical context.

<hr>

## 6. The fallen powers
Not every nation that dominated the early era has kept that up. The chart compares the average medals per Games for the top 10 early-era nations looking at results up to and including the 1980 verus their average since 1994. Countries are sorted left to right by the size of their drop off. 

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Figure 5: Average medals per Games for the top 10 pre-1980 nations, comparing their early era performance against the modern era. The collapse of URS and GDR after the Cold War is the most striking feature."
#| label: fig-fallen
cy_all = final.groupby(['year', 'noc'], as_index=False).agg(total_medals=('noc', 'count'))

early = cy_all[cy_all['year'] <= 1980]
modern = cy_all[cy_all['year'] >= 1994]
early_avg = early.groupby('noc')['total_medals'].mean().rename('early_avg').reset_index()
modern_avg = modern.groupby('noc')['total_medals'].mean().rename('modern_avg').reset_index()

top_early = early_avg.nlargest(10, 'early_avg')['noc']
compare = pd.merge(early_avg[early_avg['noc'].isin(top_early)], modern_avg, on='noc', how='left').fillna(0)
compare['decline'] = compare['early_avg'] - compare['modern_avg']
compare = compare.sort_values('decline', ascending=False)

long = compare.melt(id_vars=['noc'], value_vars=['early_avg', 'modern_avg'], var_name='era', value_name='avg_medals')
long['era'] = long['era'].map({'early_avg': 'Pre-1980 average', 'modern_avg': 'Post-1992 average'})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.barplot(data=long, x='noc', y='avg_medals', hue='era', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Early Dominance vs Modern Performance\n(Top 10 Pre-1980 Nations)', fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Country')
ax.set_ylabel('Average Medals per Games')
ax.legend(title='Era')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The biggest declines are URS (The Soviet Union) and GDR (East Germany), which may have been as a result of political and economic disruptions following the Cold War which had lasting impacts on state funded sports programmes. Norway and the US on the other hand have held their ground or improved, which probably comes down to continued investment in winter sport at a national level. This political context is something a simple GDP regression cannot capture, and it is worth keeping in mind when interpreting the model results in the next section. 

With wealth, home advantage and historical era all explored individually, the final step is combining them in a single model to estimate their effects simultaneously.

<hr>

## 7. The full picture: regression results

### 7.1 The model
With the data aggregated to country-year level, an OLS regression was run to directly answer the research question: after controlling for GDP per capita and population size, does hosting the Games have a significant impact on medal success? The model is:

>**Total Medals = β₀ + β₁(is_host) + β₂(log GDP per capita) + β₃(log population) + ε**
```{python}
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
reg_data = (pd.read_csv('../data/clean/medals_country_year.csv'))
results_df = (pd.read_csv('../outputs/regression_results.csv'))
print(f"Rows in regression dataset: {len(reg_data)}")
print(f"Countries represented: {reg_data['noc'].nunique()}")
print(f"Host-country observations: {reg_data['is_host'].sum()}")
print()
print(results_df.to_string(index=False))

model = smf.ols('total_medals ~ is_host + log_gdp_per_capita + log_population', data=reg_data).fit()
print(model.summary())
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "Table 2: Basic OLS vs country fixed effects with only key coefficients"

comparison = pd.read_csv('../outputs/model_comparison.csv')
comparison.style.set_caption('Table 2: Basic OLS vs Country Fixed Effects')
```

The table above puts the two models next to each other. The basic OLS shows the raw associations between each predictor and medal counts. The fixed effects version asks a harder question, not "whether host countries win more overall" but rather "whether a country wins more than it normally would when it hosts". 

The `is_host` coefficient drops from 4.6 in the basic OLS to 4.0 in the fixed effects model, a small decrease, but importantly it remains positive and significant ( p = 0.001, stronger than the basic OLS p = 0.015). This means the hosting boost is not just because strong countries tend to host. Even after comparing each country to its own typical performance, there's still a real boost from being on home soil.

The `log_gdp_per_capita` coefficient drops more substantially, from 2.664 to 1.015, which makes sense as once we control for country-specific differences, a lot of the gap in wealth between countries is already accounted for. The `log_population` coefficient in the fixed effects model is unusually large and should be interpreted with caution, as population doesn't change much within a country over time, so the model doesn't have much variation to work with. The basic OLS estimate of 1.434 is the more reliable figure for population in this case.

:::{.callout-note}
## What fixed effects adds
The basic OLS `is_host` coefficient of 4.6 compares the host country-years against all other country-years. Whereas the fixed effects estimate of 4.0 ( p = 0.001) compares a country against its own historical performance, which is a stricter test that hosting effect passes. 
:::

:::{.callout-important}
## Hosting advantage is real
The basic OLS model estimates that host nations win on average **4.6 more medals** than expected after controlling for wealth and population(p = 0.015). The country fixed effects model puts this at **4.0 extra medals** ( p = 0.001), which is a smaller but more credible estimate, as it compares each nation against its own baseline rather than against all other countries.
:::

### 7.2 What the coefficients show
The coefficient plot below pulls together all three results in one view. All three bars fall to the right of zero, meaning hosting, wealth and population all increase expected medal counts. The host status bar looks the longest, but section 8 explains why that is slightly misleading. 
```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Figure 6: OLS regression coefficients for the three predictors of Winter Olympic medal success. All bars fall to the right of zero, confirming each factor has a positive impact on medal counts. The error bars show the uncertainty around each estimate, and GDP per capita stands out as the most consistent and reliable predictor due to it having the tightest interval."
#| label: fig-regression

key_vars = ['is_host', 'log_gdp_per_capita', 'log_population']
reg_plot = reg[reg['variable'].isin(key_vars)].copy()
reg_plot['variable'] = reg_plot['variable'].map({'is_host': 'Host Country', 'log_gdp_per_capita': 'Log GDP per Capita', 'log_population': 'Log Population'})
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
ax.barh(reg_plot['variable'], reg_plot['Coef.'])
ax.errorbar(reg_plot['Coef.'], reg_plot['variable'], xerr=[
        reg_plot['Coef.'] - reg_plot['[0.025'],
        reg_plot['0.975]'] - reg_plot['Coef.']],fmt='none',color='black',capsize=4)

ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_title("What Predicts Winter Olympic Success?", fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Regression Coefficient (extra medals per unit increase)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The black error bars show the 95% confidence intervals, so the shorter the error bar, the more precisely that effect has been estimated. GDP per capita has the tightest interval of the three, indicating it is the most reliably estimated of the three. The host status bar is wider, reflecting the smaller number of hosting observations in the data. 

With all three effects estimated together, we can now return to the question we started with.

<hr>

## 8. Conclusions
So why does Norway win so much? The regression suggests the answer is not simply wealth, Norway's GDP per capita is high but not uniquely so. It is the combination of a strong winter sports culture built over a century of competition, consistent national investment and a geography that produces competitive athletes across almost every discipline. The model captures the economic and population factors, but Norway's outlier status reflects something the regression alone cannot fully explain. 

Three factors were tested: wealth, population size and hosting the Games. All three are statistically significant predictors of medal count.

#### Host advantage is real but limited
There is definitely a home advantage, the basic OLS model estimates around 4.6 more medals (p = 0.015) and the country fixed effects model puts it at 4.0 extra medals ( p = 0.001). The fixed effects result is slightly smaller but is actually stronger statistically, as it controls for each countries own baseline rather than comparing all host nations against all non-host nations. You can see the effect clearly in the USA in 2002 (34 medals) and Norway in 1994 (26 medals). That said, this effect is quite limited, a country only hosts once in a while, so even though the boost is real, it only applies to a single Olympic Games at a time.

#### The fixed effects model confirms the result
Adding country fixed effects reduces the `is_host` coefficient from 4.6 to 4.0, but as I said above the result becomes more statistically significant (p = 0.001 vs p = 0.015). The GDP per capita coefficient falls from 2.664 to 1.015 in the fixed effects model, as much of the between-country variation in wealth is absorbed by the country dummies themselves. The population coefficient in the fixed effects model should be treated with caution, as population changes very little within a country over time, leaving the model with limited information to estimate it reliably.

#### GDP per capita is the strongest overall predictor
From the regression, a 1 unit increase in log GDP per capita is associated with about 2.7 extra medals (p < 0.001). Across the full range of countries in the dataset, this adds up to roughly a 16-medal difference between the poorest and richest nations, reflecting the investment wealthy nations make in training infrastructure, coaching and athlete development.

#### Why the host bar looks largest on the chart but isn't the strongest predictor
On the coefficient plot, the host effect looks larger than GDP, which is misleading. The host coefficient (4.6) represents a one-time jump from not hosting to hosting. GDP on the other hand, applies to a variable that spans 6 units in the data, meaning its total real world effect (around 16 medals) is more than three times larger. The t-statistic for GDP (8.01) is also more than three times that of host status (2.44), confirming it is the more consistent and reliable predictor across the dataset.

#### Population matters independently of wealth
Population also plays an important role, even after accounting for wealth. A 1 unit increase in log population leads to about 1.4 additional medals (p < 0.001). Across the full range of countries, this again adds up to roughly a 16 medal difference, which is comparable in scale to the GDP effect.

:::{.callout-important}
## Bottom line 
Three things shape how well a country does at the Winter Olympics: **wealth**, **population**, and whether it is **hosting**. The OLS model estimates a hosting boost of **4.6 medals** tightening to **4.0 medals** under the stricter fixed effects test. GDP per capita has the most reliable and consistent effect across both models. So if you are trying to predict how many medals a country might win, GDP per capita on its own gets you most of the way there.
:::

<hr>
